perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Recipient or which otherwise comes to the knowledge of the Recipient, is acknowledged by the Recipient –
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Beneficiary or which otherwise comes to the knowledge of the Recipient, is acknowledged by the Recipient –
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'Recipient' to 'Beneficiary' in the section, we're creating uncertainty about who is subject to the confidentiality obligations. The agreement now inconsistently uses both terms, leading to in-text contradiction on who is considered the receiving party of the confindential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2 All Confidential Information disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Beneficiary or which otherwise comes to the knowledge of the Recipient, is acknowledged by the Recipient – 4.2.1 to be proprietary to the Disclosing Party and/or one or mo re of its Affiliates; and 4.2.2 not to confer any rights of whatsoever nature in such Confidential Information on the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Recipient' with 'Beneficiary'. The contradicted text uses both 'Beneficiary' and 'Recipient'. The Perturbation Explanation explicitly states that using both terms inconsistently creates uncertainty and an in-text contradiction regarding who is bound by the confidentiality obligations. Therefore, the change creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall be entitled to disclose the Confidential Information only to Permitted Persons, and then only to the extent that such disclosure is necessary for the Permitted Purpose and on a "need to know" basis.
perturbation.changed_text: The Beneficiary shall be entitled to disclose the Confidential Information only to Permitted Persons, and then only to the extent that such disclosure is necessary for the Permitted Purpose and on a "need to know" basis.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing Recipient with Beneficiary, it raises a dispute regarding who can disclose the confindential information, since the term 'Recipient' is the main term used in the document to refer to the receiving party of the confindential information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.3 The Recipient shall be responsible for procuring that the Permitte d Person abides by the provisions of this Agreement and agrees to be bound by the confidentiality undertakings given to the Disclosing Party by the Recipient in this Agreement. The Recipient shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Ag reeme nt by any Permitted.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Recipient' with 'Beneficiary'. The original text and contradicted text both refer to 'Recipient' as the party responsible for maintaining confidentiality and ensuring Permitted Persons also abide by the confidentiality agreement. Changing 'Recipient' to 'Beneficiary' creates a contradiction because it is unclear who is responsible for the confidentiality obligations, causing a conflict with Section 5.3, which explicitly states 'The Recipient shall be responsible...'. This meets the definition of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction, because the contract now uses multiple terms without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees that it shall protect the Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to the provisions of this Agreement using the same standard of care that it applies to safeguard its own proprietary, secret or confidential information, which shall at least be a reasonable standard of care, and that the Confidential Information shall be stored and handled in such a way as to prevent any unauthorised disclosure thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Beneficiary agrees that it shall protect the Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to the provisions of this Agreement using the same standard of care that it applies to safeguard its own proprietary, secret or confidential information, which shall at least be a reasonable standard of care, and that the Confidential Information shall be stored and handled in such a way as to prevent any unauthorised disclosure thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing Recipient with Beneficiary, introduces an in-text contradiction regarding the standard of care that must be protected to the Confidential Information, since 'Beneficiary' is not used as main reference in the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. INDEMNITY The Recipient hereby accepts ful l liability for the maintenance of the confidentiality of the Confidential Information pertaining to the Disclosing Party and hereby (in addition to, and without affecting, any other rights or remedies the Disclosing Party may have) unconditionally and irr revocably indemnifies and holds the Disclosing Party and its Affiliates harmless against any and all actions, claims, demands, liabilities, damages, costs, losses or expenses (but specifically excluding any claims for indirect or consequential loss) directl y resulting from any breach by the Recipient, or any person to whom the Recipient has disclosed or given access to any part of the Confidential Information, of any of the provisions under this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Recipient' with 'Beneficiary'. The Contradicted Text explicitly assigns liability to the 'Recipient'. By changing this to 'Beneficiary' in the Changed Text, a contradiction arises because the agreement now uses two terms interchangeably ('Recipient' and 'Beneficiary') when only 'Recipient' is explicitly liable in the indemnity clause. This introduces ambiguity regarding who is actually responsible for maintaining confidentiality, creating a strong in-text contradiction, which is a clear example of Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The undertakings given by the Recipient in this Agreement shall be for the benefit of and may be enforced by the Disclosing Party, any of its Affiliates, any current or future shareholder of the Disclosing Part y and any successors -in-title.
perturbation.changed_text: The undertakings given by the Beneficiary in this Agreement shall be for the benefit of and may be enforced by the Disclosing Party, any of its Affiliates, any current or future shareholder of the Disclosing Part y and any successors -in-title.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Recipient' to 'Beneficiary' in the section, we're creating uncertainty about the undertakings within the agreement. The agreement now inconsistently uses both terms, leading to in-text contradiction regarding who is bound by the given undertakings.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 22.1 The undertakings given by the Beneficiary in this Agreement shall be for the benefit of and may be enforced by the Disclosing Party, any of its Affiliates, any current or future shareholder of the Disclosing Part y and any successors -in-title. The undertakings shall be deemed to have been imposed as a stipulation alteri for the benefit of any Affiliate of the Disclosing Party, any third party which becomes a shareholder in the Disclosing Party and any successor -in-title and such benefit may be accepted by such person in writing at any time. The fact that any undertaking may not be enforceable by one of them will not affect its enforceability by any other party.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation states that the change from 'Recipient' to 'Beneficiary' creates uncertainty and inconsistency within the agreement, as both terms are now used. The contradicted text uses the term 'Beneficiary'. The changed text uses the term 'Beneficiary' where previously 'Recipient' was used. This change aligns with the perturbation explanation and causes a contradiction as the agreement now inconsistently uses both terms, leading to in-text contradiction regarding who is bound by the given undertakings.
